articles.title: Saving Special Water Streams in England
articles.summary: People who care about nature want to protect chalk streams better. Chalk streams are rare freshwater habitats found mostly in England. They have clear water and gravel bottoms because they come from springs in chalky ground. There are only 220 chalk streams in the world, and most are in southern England, like the River Wandle in London. The London Wildlife Trust says these streams need more legal protection to keep them safe from harm. They want the government to include chalk streams in rules that help plan how land is used. The government says it is working to clean up water and invest money to fix chalk streams. Water companies are also making plans to improve these streams and reduce pollution.

articles.title: A Surprising Discovery in Tiny Atoms
articles.summary: Scientists have found something amazing about tiny atoms! For a long time, they thought that atoms with extra neutrons could act strangely, breaking normal rules. These strange areas are called 'Islands of Inversion.' But now, a team of scientists from around the world discovered a new Island of Inversion in a different place. They studied two types of molybdenum atoms: molybdenum-84 and molybdenum-86. These atoms have equal numbers of protons and neutrons, which is very balanced. Using special machines at Michigan State University, they shot beams of atoms and measured gamma rays to see how the atoms behaved. They found that even though the two atoms are very similar, with only two neutrons difference, they act very differently. This discovery helps us learn more about how atoms work and surprises scientists because it happened in a balanced atom, not one with extra neutrons.

articles.title: Asteroids Play Toss with Cosmic Snowballs
articles.summary: Scientists have discovered that asteroids can throw rocks and dust at each other, like playing a slow game of catch in space. This happens in binary asteroid systems, where two asteroids orbit each other. NASA's DART mission took pictures of an asteroid called Dimorphos before crashing into it. In those pictures, researchers saw bright streaks that look like fan shapes. These streaks show that material moved from one asteroid to another, which they call "cosmic snowballs." This helps scientists learn more about asteroids, especially ones that might come near Earth. The study was led by a team from the University of Maryland and published in 2026.

articles.title: Brain Signals and Autism: A Traffic Light Story
articles.summary: Scientists are studying how the brain works, comparing it to traffic lights guiding cars. They found a chemical called nitric oxide that helps brain cells talk to each other. In some kids with autism, this chemical can get stuck like a button, causing problems. When nitric oxide gets stuck, it makes a protective protein called TSC2 disappear. TSC2 normally helps control a system called mTOR, which manages cell growth. Without TSC2, mTOR can become too active, like a car going too fast. The good news is that scientists can block this stuck button, helping cells return to normal. This discovery might lead to new ways to help people with autism in the future.

articles.title: How AI Helps Find Better Batteries
articles.summary: Scientists are working on new batteries called all-solid-state batteries that are safer and can hold more energy than the batteries we use today. These batteries need special materials that let tiny particles called ions move quickly through them. Finding these materials is hard because it takes a lot of time and computer power. Researchers have used artificial intelligence (AI) to help solve this problem. They created a smart computer program that can predict when ions move in a liquid-like way inside solid materials by looking at light signals called Raman spectra. This method helps find materials that make ions move fast, which is important for better batteries. It works by simulating how materials behave at high temperatures without needing too much computer power. This discovery could lead to faster development of new battery technologies.

articles.title: A Dinosaur with a Head Spike Found in the Desert
articles.summary: Scientists found a new dinosaur called Spinosaurus in the Sahara Desert. This dinosaur had a big spike on its head and ate fish. Before, people thought Spinosaurus could dive deep in the ocean like in movies, but the new fossils show it lived near rivers far from the sea. The scientists, led by Paul Sereno, discovered the bones in Niger with help from local guides. They think Spinosaurus was too buoyant to dive because its bones had air sacks, like birds. Instead, it probably waded in shallow water to catch fish, similar to a heron. This finding changes what we know about how Spinosaurus lived and hunted.
